rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL VS LAIDLAW TRANSIT INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant First Student, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Evidence Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday May 27, 2015, Line 1, Defendant First Student, Inc.'s Motion For Evidence Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Pro Tem David McDonald recuses himself from this matter. Continued to 6/10/15 at 9:00 a.m. in Department 301. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JORDANNE HO-SHUE et al VS. GDL SFO, INC. DBA GOLD DUST LOUNGE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant Gdl Sfo, Inc. To Compel Plaintiff Jordanne Ho-Shues Further Responses To Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday May 27, 2015, Line 3, DEFENDANT GDL SFO, INC.'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Jordanne Ho-Shues Further Responses To Discovery. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted in part; denied in part; and continued for further hearing on 6/15/15 at 9:00 a.m. To the extent it has not previously been provided, Plaintiff Ho-Shue shall verify the 2nd further responses by May 29, 2015. The Court finds that Plaintiff's responses to Interrogatory Nos. 1 and RFPD Nos. 4 and 5 are sufficient and no further responses are required. The Court finds that Plaintiff's response to Interrogatory No. 4 is insufficient and a further response must be provided. Additionally, the Court finds that Plaintiff's responses to Interrogatory Nos. 2, 3, 8, and 9 are partially defective. While Plaintiff need not provide the level of detail argued by Defendants [that information can be obtained in follow up interrogatories], Plaintiff must supplement the responses to indicate (to the best of her knowledge and information available to her) the numbers of hours she actually worked on each day versus the hours actually paid by Defendants. As for Interrogatory Nos. 5 and 6, Plaintiff shall supplemental to state what monies were owed and not paid and Defendant's willfulness in not making such payments. Plaintiff shall provide further verified supplemental responses by email no later than 6/8/15. A copy such responses shall also be sent to Pro Tem McDonald. The parties may, but are not required to, submit a short supplemental brief by 6/10/15. The Court will hold a further hearing on 6/15/15 to assess compliance with the Court's order and any remaining issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA ZISKOVSKY VS. MARIN HEALTHCARE DISTRICT ET AL***CASE TRANSFERRED TO MARIN COUNTY***
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Change Venue; Request For Costs And Fees; Marin Healthcare Districts Motion To Change Venue To Marin County; Request For Costs And Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 27, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANTS MARIN HEALTHCARE DISTRICT, AND MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL'S Motion To Change Venue; Request For Costs And Fees. Off calendar as duplicate entry. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA ZISKOVSKY VS. MARIN HEALTHCARE DISTRICT ET AL***CASE TRANSFERRED TO MARIN COUNTY***
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion To Change Venue; Request For Costs And Fees; Mrin Healthcare Districts Motion To Change Venue To Marin County; Request For Costs And Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 27, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANTS MARIN HEALTHCARE DISTRICT, AND MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL'S Amended Motion To Change Venue; Request For Costs And Fees. Granted. This action is transferred to Marin County. Marin Healthcare District is a public entity as a matter of law. Gov. Code § 7530 provides that "districts" are public entities for all purposes. Further, the Health & Safety Code governs healthcare districts, and provides that they are established by the approval of district's voters, they are supported by taxes from residents in the district, and their governing boards are elected by district residents. (Health & Safety Code, §§ 32000 et seq.) The Health & Safety Code also provides that in order to make a claim against a healthcare district, the Government Claim Act procedures must be followed. (Id., § 32492.) Additionally, the complaint alleges that it served a "timely government claim." (Complaint, par. 4.) Although Plaintiff now argues that it "prophylactically filed claims" just in case Marin Healthcare District was a public agency, there is nothing in the complaint that indicates that Plaintiff questioned Marin Healthcare District's public entity status. Marin Healthcare District also submits the Declaration of Leo Domanico in reply, which establishes that Marin Healthcare District is a public entity. (Declaration of Leo Domanico, par. 2.) Plaintiff may respond to this evidence orally at the hearing if she chooses to do so. Pursuant to CCP sec. 396(b), Plaintiff shall pay Defendants reasonable attorney's fees and costs in the amount of $540. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Part 2 Of 2 For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 27, 2015, Line 6, Petitioner's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Part 2 Of 2 For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) Defendant cites Sprint PCS Assets v. City of Palos Verdes Estates (9th Cir. 2009) 583F.3d 716 ("Palos Verdes"), and argues that it is allowed to regulate the public sidewalk and promote aesthetics as part of its police powers. The court agrees with this assertion, but defendant overstates the import of Palos Verdes. In Palos Verdes, the local municipality denied two permits for wireless telecommunication facilities because one of the facilities was to be constructed on a narrow residential street and the other was to be placed at one of the main entrances to the municipality. The court held that with regard to the two specific permit applications, substantial evidence supported the municipality's decision to deny the applications. The court explained that the municipality properly considered aesthetics as part of the time, place, and manner regulatory scheme. The instant case is distinguishable. SF Public Works Code sec. 2712(d)(4) requires plaintiff to make "reasonable efforts" to obtain an easement over private property as a prerequisite to seeking a permit in all instances. Plaintiff must make those "reasonable efforts" whether the proposed surface mounted facility ("SMF") is in a residential neighborhood, in an industrial neighborhood, the size of a shoebox, or the size of a car. Defendant's one-size-fits-all approach and general dislike of SMFs in the public right of way conflict with plaintiff's rights under PUC 7901 and 5885. See City of Ranchos Palos Verdes v. Abrams (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 367, 381, (holding that "generalized concerns about aesthetics are insufficient" to deny a permit to construct a cell phone tower). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of Tenative Ruling) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 27, 2015, Line 6, Petitioner's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling). The court grants the motion for judgment on the pleadings without leave to amend. Plaintiff is entitled to a declaration that Public Utilities Code secs. ("PUC") 7901 and 5885 preempt SF Public Works Code ("PWC") sec. 2712(d)(4). As an initial matter, the court notes that both parties appear to agree that the court can resolve this case on the pleadings even though defendant's answer denies plaintiff's allegations and includes numerous conclusory affirmative defenses. Resolution of this case is a legal matter and involves a comparison of PUC 7091/5885 and PWC 2712(d)(4) to determine preemption. "Local legislation is 'contradictory' to general law when it is inimical thereto." Sherwin-Williams Co. v. City of Los Angeles (1993) 4 Cal.4th 893, 898 [citation omitted]. A local law contradicts state law where it "mandate[s] what state law expressly forbids," or "forbid[s] what state law expressly mandates." Great Western Shows, Inc. v. County of Los Angeles (2002) 27 Cal.4th 853, 866. In County of Los Angeles v. General Telephone Co. of Cal. (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 903, 907, the court explained that the purpose of PUC 7901 was to encourage development of a state-wide telephone network and "to spare companies the expense of acquiring easements over privately owned land." SF Public Works Code sec. 2712(d)(4) and its related regulations are inimical to this purpose. The municipal code obligates the telecommunications provider as a prerequisite to seeking to install a SMF to first seek an easement. The "reasonable efforts" that the provider is to make (e.g., identification of properties, mailings, offer of market rate compensation, etc.) impose a prohibitive price on the exercise of plaintiff's rights under the Public Utilities Code. See Apartment Ass'n of Los Angeles County v. City of Los Angeles (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 119, 133. The requirement to pay a fee to property owners is also contrary to Los Angeles County v. General Tel. Co. of Cal., (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 903. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: ARKADY BERGER VS. GARY VARUM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 27, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT ALEX VARUM'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Overruled. Defendant's request for judicial notice is granted. In order for collateral estoppel to apply, the issue "must have been actually litigated in the former proceeding." (South Sutter LLC v. LJ Sutter Partners, L.P. (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 634, 661-662.) Although the Second Amended Complaint in the underlying action generally alleges at paragraph seven that Defendant Alex Varum is the alter ego of Gary Varum and Telesis, there is nothing in the judicially noticeable documents to indicate that this issue was "actually litigated." The tentative decision and final judgment are silent on the issue of alter ego as to Alex Varum. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
